b' Money Stuff
Here is a trend in modern finance: Banks have a fundamentally risky business model, borrowing short (by taking deposits) and lending long. I

SRT-squared

Here is a trend in modern finance:

  1. Banks have a fundamentally risky business model, borrowing short (by taking deposits) and lending long.
  2. In some ways it would be nice if, instead of banks with risky short-term deposits, somebody else, somebody with long-term funding, did all the risky lending. Banks could take deposits (a risky funding model), and then invest them very safely; other investors could raise long-term equity funding (a safe funding model), and use it to make risky loans and investments. Loosely speaking, this is called \xe2\x80\x9c narrow banking .\xe2\x80\x9d
  3. And in fact, these days, there are regulatory and market pressures on banks to take fewer of the risks they have traditionally taken. There are stricter capital and liquidity requirements (so banks can\xe2\x80\x99t fund their risky loans too much with risky deposits), and prudential pressures on banks to get out of their riskier businesses (proprietary trading, leveraged lending). The banks take less risk.
  4. And in fact, these days, people do go out and raise safe long-term funding to take some of the risks that banks have traditionally taken, in part because those regulatory and market pressures have made banks a bit less willing to take them. Hedge funds and proprietary trading firms now do some of the market-making functions that banks used to do . Private credit firms now do a lot of the risky balance-sheet lending that banks used to do. And banks now routinely sell some of their credit risk to hedge funds and other investors, essentially buying insurance from those firms against the risk that their loans will default.
  5. Great!
  6. All these people with their long-term funds have a problem, though. The reason these businesses work, for banks, is leverage . If you are a bank doing a trade with $10 of shareholders\xe2\x80\x99 equity and $90 of deposits, and the trade pays $2, that\xe2\x80\x99s great: You pay roughly $0 to depositors, so that $2 is a 20% return on equity. But if you are a hedge fund doing that same trade with $100 of your own investors\xe2\x80\x99 money, it\xe2\x80\x99s not great: That $2 is a 2% return on equity.
  7. What you want, if you are an investor with safe stable long-term funding doing these trades that banks used to do, is leverage . You want to borrow 90% of the money to do the trade, preferably cheaply, so that you can get a good return on equity for the people giving you that safe stable long-term funding.
  8. Where can you borrow all that money?
  9. Well, from banks, obviously. They have lots of money! They are in the business of making loans! It just makes sense.
  10. This sort of takes us back to Step 1, doesn\xe2\x80\x99t it? The risky business leaves the banking system and goes to people with long-term stable funding, but then it comes back into the banking system because those people lever up their bets with bank loans.

So we have talked about how banks\xe2\x80\x99 trading divisions are increasingly in the business of lending money to hedge funds and proprietary trading firms, who then do the market-making and trading functions that the banks used to do themselves. And we have talked a few times about how private credit funds sometimes lever up their own investors\xe2\x80\x99 money by getting financing from banks. The risky business itself \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0proprietary trading, financing leveraged buyouts \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0is shifted from the banks to other firms, but the other firms turn around and borrow money from the banks to help fund it.

Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s\xc2\xa0Esteban Duarte, Donal Griffin\xc2\xa0and Carmen Arroyo report on synthetic risk transfers :

When JPMorgan Chase & Co. arranged a series of trades to shift the risk of losses from $20 billion of its loans, some of those dangers wound up at a familiar place: rival banks.

The deal, struck late last year, was one of the biggest ever Synthetic Risk Transfer trades, or SRTs, opaque transactions heralded by Wall Street and approved by regulators that are supposed to hand possible loan losses to hedge funds and other nonbank investors. Yet some buyers in the JPMorgan deal \xe2\x80\x94 and in multiple other SRT trades \xe2\x80\x94 borrowed money from other banks to help finance their stakes and inflate returns, people familiar with the matter say.

Nomura Holdings Inc., Morgan Stanley and NatWest Group Plc, have emerged as some of the most active lenders to investors in SRTs, along with rivals including Banco Santander SA and Standard Chartered Plc. That leaves them well-placed to profit from an expected surge in the deals \xe2\x80\x94 but exposed to potential losses if debts end up going bad and the SRT investor hits trouble.

The lurking presence of Wall Street loans behind some of these complex trades suggests exposures that were meant to be shifted elsewhere remain tied to the banking system, an outcome that\xe2\x80\x99s starting to spook regulators.

\xe2\x80\x9cIf a bank\xe2\x80\x99s lending against the SRT instrument as collateral, you\xe2\x80\x99re clearly not transferring the risk outside the banking system,\xe2\x80\x9d says Sheila Bair, who led the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. during the financial crisis. \xe2\x80\x9cAny counterparty investing in SRT using bank-provided leverage should be prohibited, full stop.\xe2\x80\x9d

The rough idea of an SRT is that a bank buys insurance against the risk of defaults in some portfolio of its loans. Normally the way to do this is by issuing credit-linked notes: The bank sells bonds that reference the underlying portfolio of loans, and if those loans default then it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have to pay back the bonds. In exchange for this protection, the bank pays a high\xc2\xa0interest rate on the bonds. This is worth it, to the bank, because it reduces its capital requirements: Paying double-digit interest rates to move risk off its balance sheet is a good deal for the bank, because it would be even more expensive to keep the risk and meet the accompanying capital requirements.\xc2\xa0The buyers of these bonds \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the people taking the credit risk off the bank\xe2\x80\x99s books \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0are \xe2\x80\x9cfirms such as Ares Management Corp., Blackstone and Magnetar Capital,\xe2\x80\x9d investment firms that raise long-term money from investors and are thus less strictly regulated than banks.

And then the buyers go and borrow money from the banks:

To help pay for these trades, some firms will seek outside financing. Hedge funds usually do so through a class of bank lending known as repurchase agreements, or \xe2\x80\x9crepos,\xe2\x80\x9d in which the lender takes the investing fund\xe2\x80\x99s SRT securities as collateral, according to people with knowledge of the practice.

Others use different types of bank borrowing such as net asset-value \xe2\x80\x94 or NAV \xe2\x80\x94 loans, a kind of financing popular with private equity firms that\xe2\x80\x99s secured against a portfolio of their holdings. To back its JPMorgan deal, LuminArx used a non-repo form of borrowing, a person familiar with the matter says.

While European SRT buyers haven\xe2\x80\x99t used much leverage in the recent past, the trade\xe2\x80\x99s economics are different on the other side of the Atlantic where borrowed money is often needed to deliver stellar returns. \xe2\x80\x9cLeverage in various forms has been employed by most investors\xe2\x80\x9d on recent US SRT deals, says Alan Shaffran, senior portfolio manager at Magnetar.

European and Canadian SRTs are commonly made up of only the riskiest portion of a bank\xe2\x80\x99s loans, which offers the best returns. But the US equivalents are broader \xe2\x80\x94 or \xe2\x80\x9cthicker\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 because the buyers have to insure larger portions of the debt, meaning the securities they buy yield less. That creates an incentive to use borrowed money to juice profit.

If an investment fund just uses its own cash for a US SRT trade, it can make loss-adjusted returns in the high single digits, market participants say. When the booster shot of leverage is added, returns can jump to the mid-teens.

Well, right, you need leverage. Schematically,\xc2\xa0the capital structure is something like [1] :

  1. JPMorgan makes $100 of loans and does an SRT.
  2. Magnetar \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the equity buyer of the SRT \xe2\x80\x94 puts up, say, $5 to buy the SRT, and takes the first $5 of losses on those loans: If $1 worth of loans default, Magnetar only gets $4\xc2\xa0back from its $5 investment.
  3. Nomura \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the repo lender on the SRT \xe2\x80\x94 puts up, say, $7\xc2\xa0of repo leverage to help Magnetar buy the SRT, and takes the next $7\xc2\xa0of losses on those loans: If $10 worth of loans default, Magnetar loses its entire $5 equity investment, but it also can\xe2\x80\x99t pay back Nomura\xe2\x80\x99s repo loan, and Nomura loses $5 of its $7. [2]
  4. JPMorgan keeps the last $88 of losses: If $20\xc2\xa0worth of loans default, Magnetar and Nomura are wiped out and JPMorgan bears $8 of losses.

Some risk has been transferred out of the banking system altogether \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the equity investor in the SRT really does take the first losses \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but some of it has been recycled back into another bit of the banking system.

Obviously the next move is for a bank to package up a bunch of its SRT repo loans and issue SRTs against them. An SRT-squared, what could possibly etc.

No, I kid, I love it, this is just how the world works. I wrote about the trading desks :

It is striking how normal this is. \xe2\x80\xa6 Broadly speaking, in the economy, there are all sorts of businesses that do stuff , and banks are in the meta-business of lending them money to do the stuff . That meta-business is supposed to be safer than the underlying businesses: Instead of being in risky businesses themselves, banks have a diversified collection of senior claims on risky businesses; the other businesses run the risks, and the banks get the relatively safe first claim on their revenues.

It turns out that this is true even of the business of taking risks off the books of banks.

Paramount

The basic situation at Paramount Global is that National Amusements Inc., a company controlled by Shari Redstone, owns roughly 5% of Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s total stock, but roughly 77% of the voting stock. If you want to acquire Paramount, what should you do? One option is to go to Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board of directors and propose a merger in which you pay cash to acquire all of the stock at a premium; the market capitalization of the stock right now is about $8 billion, so this might require you to pay perhaps $10 billion or $12 billion.

Another option, though, is to buy National Amusements\xe2\x80\x99 stake. Again, National Amusements owns about 5% of the stock; if Paramount is worth $8 billion, then 5% of it is worth about $400 million. In fact the voting Class A stock trades at a higher price than the nonvoting Class B stock, and you\xe2\x80\x99d have to pay Redstone a premium, but still. Maybe you pay $1 billion or so for that stock. Or maybe you buy National Amusements outright from Redstone \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0it also owns some movie theaters \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and pay $2 billion or so to get the Paramount stock plus the movie theaters. And then you control 77% of the Paramount votes. And then you fire the board and take control and do what you want.

Well. You don\xe2\x80\x99t really fire the board and take control and do whatever you want. Corporate law imposes some restrictions on what a controlling shareholder like National Amusements can do with a public company like Paramount; if you bought National Amusements, you\xe2\x80\x99d have some fiduciary duties to other shareholders, and anything you did would be closely watched by those shareholders and the courts. And Paramount has a board of directors that is separate from National Amusements, with its own fiduciary duties to the nonvoting shareholders, and that board can make trouble. (It once tried to take away Redstone\xe2\x80\x99s voting control !)

So a third option is that you could go to National Amusements and offer to buy it, but simultaneously negotiate with Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board of directors to try to get an all-around deal. Because what you really want is to acquire Paramount. Acquiring 77% of the voting stock of Paramount gets you a long way toward that goal, but not all the way: You still have a board, judicial oversight, minority shareholders, etc. You want Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board and shareholders to endorse your plans before you shell out billions of dollars for National Amusements.

The Paramount deal talks over the last few months have involved a consortium of bidders (Apollo Global Management and Sony Pictures Entertainment) trying roughly the first approach , talking to Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board about buying the whole company for cash, and another consortium (Skydance Media, Redbird Capital and KKR & Co.) trying roughly the second approach , talking to Redstone about buying National Amusements but conditioning the deal on reaching an understanding with the Paramount board. That requires some compromises: Skydance has to give the public shareholders something to win over the Paramount board, and whatever value it gives them comes out of Redstone\xe2\x80\x99s pocket. And in fact last week Skydance seemed to have reached a deal with Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board , in which it would buy National Amusements and also pay some cash out to the public shareholders.

But the ultimate fact of Paramount is that Redstone does control 77% of the votes, and while she can\xe2\x80\x99t do whatever she wants, she can block whatever she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want. So the Wall Street Journal reports :

Shari Redstone \xe2\x80\xa6 has ended discussions to sell her controlling stake in Paramount Global to Skydance Media and merge the two companies, drawing to a close months of negotiations in one of the messiest deal dramas to play out in recent years.

National Amusements, the Redstone family company that controls Paramount, said the parties had not been able to reach a deal that would have given Skydance the keys to one of Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s most storied brands. The statement confirmed news first reported by The Wall Street Journal.

Redstone\xe2\x80\x99s decision marks a stunning about-face, after she advocated behind the scenes for months for a deal with Skydance. She billed its backers\xe2\x80\x94including CEO David Ellison\xe2\x80\x94as the best stewards for Paramount, owner of the namesake film studio, broadcaster CBS and cable channels such as MTV and Nickelodeon.

In the end, Redstone had a change of heart, as trust frayed between the two sides. Redstone will now likely pursue a sale of just National Amusements, without trying to merge Paramount into another company, people close to her said.

In some ways that is the simplest solution. Reaching a deal that simultaneously satisfies Redstone and Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s board is very tricky; every penny you give Redstone comes at the expense of public shareholders and vice versa. Reaching a deal just to sell National Amusements is easy: That only has to satisfy Redstone. And then dealing with Paramount is the buyer's problem.

Should index funds be illegal?

We talked last month about a Federal Trade Commission ruling involving Scott Sheffield, the former chief executive officer of Pioneer Natural Resources. Exxon Mobil Corp. had a deal to buy Pioneer, and the FTC demanded, as a condition of approving the deal, that Sheffield not be allowed to serve on Exxon\xe2\x80\x99s board. The FTC claimed that Sheffield conspired with the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries to keep oil prices high: Sheffield had some meetings and communications with OPEC officials, and he also went around making public statements that US oil companies like Pioneer should be more disciplined about capital expenditures and drilling. Instead of rushing to drill more wells every time oil prices went up, they should moderate production so they could actually profit from those high prices.

This view, I should say, was (and is) not at all uncommon, and you do not need a conspiracy to explain it. The US oil industry had quite well-known cycles\xc2\xa0of overspending during booms and then collapsing in busts; investing in oil drillers was a good way to lose money. The companies knew this, the shareholders knew this, and it just made sense for them all to rethink things. Instead of losing money on oil drilling, wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t it be nicer to make money? The way you do this is by not investing too much in new capacity all the time. Everyone could have come to that conclusion on their own, without conspiring with OPEC, or with each other.

Last month Sheffield filed\xc2\xa0a comment with the FTC , objecting to how he was treated in the Exxon settlement. His basic complaints, which strike me as pretty fair, are (1) the\xc2\xa0FTC\xe2\x80\x99s claims\xc2\xa0about his ties to OPEC are false or exaggerated and (2) he didn\xe2\x80\x99t have any opportunity to respond to them, because the FTC\xe2\x80\x99s criticism of him came in a settlement with Exxon without his involvement. But he also defends his decisions to moderate production by saying they were informed, not by conversations with OPEC, but by conversations with BlackRock:

Pioneer\xe2\x80\x99s new capital framework was informed by discussions with shareholders and Pioneer\xe2\x80\x99s Investor Relations Department, which tracked shareholder and analyst feedback and other public reports. Within the first month of returning as CEO, Mr. Sheffield traveled the country speaking with nearly three dozen of Pioneer\xe2\x80\x99s largest shareholders (comprising more than one-third of the outstanding shares at the time), including major investment managers like Fidelity, TIAA/Nuveen, Capital World Investors, and Blackrock. These firms were managing trillions of dollars for pension and retirement funds and others.

Nearly all of the shareholders with whom Mr. Sheffield spoke expressed support for moderating growth to improve Pioneer\xe2\x80\x99s free cash flow, strengthen its balance sheet, and lower costs and execution risk. Pioneer and Mr. Sheffield frequently received questions from shareholders on these topics during road shows and conferences. The decision to return more free cash flow to shareholders was undertaken to provide shareholders the level of returns that they expected regardless of oil prices, not to impact oil prices or output. These issues became even more critical when COVID hit.

There are numerous industry reports documenting the shift in how analysts and shareholders were thinking about the oil industry around the time of Mr. Sheffield\xe2\x80\x99s return as CEO. One report published by Wolfe Research in 2017 explains that \xe2\x80\x9c[s]hareholders are demanding cash return.\xe2\x80\x9d The analyst firm says, \xe2\x80\x9cWe implore companies to exert capital discipline and temper growth targets in the pursuit of cash returns. We will resolutely oppose aggressive growth targets and strategies, in general.\xe2\x80\x9d The report explains that \xe2\x80\x9coil stocks dramatically underperformed the market\xe2\x80\x9d because of concerns about the \xe2\x80\x9cEnd of the Oil Age.\xe2\x80\x9d These demands were not hidden from the public or government authorities. Major news organizations covered them.\xc2\xa0Mr. Sheffield regularly discussed his views on Pioneer\xe2\x80\x99s capital framework given shareholder interest in it.

Again! If you had asked Fidelity, TIAA, Capital and BlackRock \xe2\x80\x9cthinking only from your perspective as shareholders of Pioneer , ignoring everything else, should Pioneer drill more oil or return cash,\xe2\x80\x9d I bet they would all have said \xe2\x80\x9creturn cash.\xe2\x80\x9d The boom-and-bust cycle was well understood at that point, and bad for shareholders; returning cash was the rational thing to do.

And yet\xc2\xa0those shareholders were not shareholders only of Pioneer: They are giant institutions who own shares in many oil companies. It\xe2\x80\x99s possible that a Pioneer-only shareholder would have said \xe2\x80\x9cno, let all the other companies cut production; Pioneer should invest more in drilling to grab market share while everyone else is cutting back.\xe2\x80\x9d That is a possibly rational thing to think if you are a shareholder of one oil company, but an insane thing to think if you are a shareholder of every oil company: If everyone increases production, no one gains market share and the price of oil goes down. If all of these shareholders were meeting with Sheffield on the same day they were meeting with other oil CEOs, of course they expressed support for moderating growth.

You know the theory : Common ownership of all the companies in an industry, by a handful of big diversified institutional investors, leads to reduced competition over price. This theory is hotly controversial, in part because no one believes that diversified investors go to meetings with CEOs and say \xe2\x80\x9cyou should cut production to keep prices high.\xe2\x80\x9d But here is a CEO almost saying that, as a defense to charges that OPEC told him \xe2\x80\x9cyou should cut production to keep prices high.\xe2\x80\x9d It wasn\xe2\x80\x99t OPEC, it was the mutual funds.

Fake family office

The most and least prestigious job in finance is \xe2\x80\x9cI manage my family\xe2\x80\x99s investments.\xe2\x80\x9d One thing that it can mean is that you had a long (or, better, short) and lucrative career as a professional investor, in which you learned all the skills you need to pick investments that go up, and also made so much money that you don\xe2\x80\x99t need to work at a job anymore. Instead, you took the money you earned at your job, used it to buy\xc2\xa0more investments that went up, got even wealthier, and now fund your lifestyle by picking investments for an hour a week and otherwise play golf and sail boats.

Another thing it can mean is that you are just regular unemployed, but you\xe2\x80\x99ve got $200 in a Robinhood account, and you use that to YOLO GameStop options. [3]

If you are in the latter category, though, and when people ask you what you do you say \xe2\x80\x9cI manage my family\xe2\x80\x99s investment portfolio,\xe2\x80\x9d you will probably get into a few more parties than if you said\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m unemployed so I mess around on Robinhood all day.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cI work at a family office\xe2\x80\x9d is not quite the same thing as saying \xe2\x80\x9cI manage my family\xe2\x80\x99s investments,\xe2\x80\x9d but it\xe2\x80\x99s not that far off either. Ordinarily a \xe2\x80\x9cfamily office\xe2\x80\x9d means some sort of corporate entity, not just a personal account, but who\xe2\x80\x99s going to check? And \xe2\x80\x9cfamily office\xe2\x80\x9d does imply, without saying, that your family\xe2\x80\x99s investment portfolio is pretty big.

Here\xe2\x80\x99s a fun Bloomberg News article about fake family offices :

From the conference halls of Singapore to events hosted by top Hong Kong officials, the ultra-wealthy clans that control family offices in Asia are encountering a new and bizarre problem: fake or exaggerated \xe2\x80\x98peers\xe2\x80\x99 created seemingly overnight with little or no substance.

The family office space has become so big, it\xe2\x80\x99s attracting \xe2\x80\x9clegitimate players but also people trying to make a quick buck or hustle,\xe2\x80\x9d said Collevecchio, the managing director of Oppenheimer Generations Asia, a family office that represents a branch of the clan that once controlled diamond giant De Beers. ...

\xe2\x80\x9cThis industry lends itself to frauds\xe2\x80\x9d since it\xe2\x80\x99s built on secrecy, said Francois Botha, founder of Denmark-based Simple, which services family offices. \xe2\x80\x9cNobody checks the credentials \xe2\x80\x94 there\xe2\x80\x99s quite a lack of due diligence.\xe2\x80\x9d

I do not entirely understand the point of pretending to work at a family office, but I gather there are two main use cases. One is that you pretend to be a sophisticated investor controlling a large pile of wealth, and then use the trust that that engenders with other family offices to swindle them into co-investing with you in some fraud that you have devised. The other is going to parties:

For some, it\xe2\x80\x99s a way to join the in-crowd, getting free access to conferences that would otherwise cost thousands of dollars. As one private equity investor who had encountered several fakes put it: \xe2\x80\x9cthere is no party like a free conference party.\xe2\x80\x9d

I guess that's true, though I feel like there are better parties than a free conference party?

Things happen

Elon Musk Drops Suit Accusing OpenAI of Breaching Founding Mission. Elon Musk\xe2\x80\x99s Boundary-Blurring Relationships With Women at SpaceX. BlackRock Scouts for Insurance Partnerships in Private-Debt Push. Credit-Market Crowding Forces Nonbank Lenders to Get Creative. McKinsey Boss\xe2\x80\x99s Next Big Consulting Project: His Own Firm. US Money Funds Look Past Reform Angst Thanks to High Rates. \xe2\x80\x98Anti-Woke\xe2\x80\x99 Shareholders Are Going After Corporate Boards. The Tiny Trades That Brought Down Segantii\xe2\x80\x99s Giant Hedge Fund.\xc2\xa0Argentina Loses $1.5 Billion UK Appeal Over GDP Linked Bonds .\xc2\xa0ECB flags euro risks from Russia as global forex reserves dip. Flavor Flav Officially Teams with Red Lobster After Attempting to Save the Bankrupt Chain on Social Media.

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] These numbers are slightly fake for ease of use. More realistically, US SRT trades seem to require a 12.5% tranche thickness for the first-loss tranche, so really JPMorgan would take $87.50 of the risk and the SRT buyer and its lenders would split the other $12.50. And then Duarte, Griffin and Arroyo report that \xe2\x80\x9cmarket participants surmise that anywhere between 10% and 50% of the money for these deals could be borrowed,\xe2\x80\x9d and that banks \xe2\x80\x9ccan typically provide up to 60% of the value of a trade through repo financing.\xe2\x80\x9d So the lender would put up anywhere from $1.25 to $7.50 of the money, with the SRT buyer putting up the rest.

[2] This is loose, and in fact the repo lender can issue margin calls if the loans start to default and hopefully protect itself against this risk.

[3] A third thing it can mean is that your parent or grandparent earned a ton of money, you got an MBA, and now you take monthly meetings with a staff of professional wealth managers. This is somewhere between the other two options in prestige, but honestly much closer to the former.
